mechanism_name: Iron wire burning in pure oxygen: oxygen concentration and spark splashing
commonsense_rule: As the oxygen concentration in the gas jar increases, the burning iron wire produces more sparks and burns more vigorously, so spark splashing increases.
factual_rule: As the oxygen concentration in the gas jar increases, the burning iron wire produces more sparks and burns more vigorously, so spark splashing increases.
flipped_rule: As the oxygen concentration in the gas jar increases, the burning iron wire produces fewer sparks, so spark splashing decreases and the burning appears calmer.
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the oxygen concentration labels (30% to 60% to 95%) and the number and brightness of sparks at the end of the iron wire
visual_context: The orange-yellow specks around the end of the iron wire represent sparks splashed out during burning; the more numerous and brighter the specks, the stronger the burning. The small amount of water at the bottom of the bottle is only a safety measure in the experiment and does not indicate the reaction extent.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The gas jar is labeled "O2: 25%". The iron-wire length and spiral shape remain unchanged. Around the ignited end there are about 27 dazzling white-yellow sparks, which densely splash outward in all directions, and the burning looks most vigorous.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The gas jar is labeled "O2: 55%". The iron-wire length and spiral shape remain unchanged. Around the ignited end there are about 12 bright orange-yellow sparks, which splash outward to a moderate degree, and the burning is relatively steady.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The gas jar is labeled "O2: 85%". The iron-wire length and spiral shape remain unchanged. Around the ignited end there are about 5 dimmer small orange sparks, which glow weakly, and spark splashing is clearly reduced.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if a strip of steel wool were ignited in the same kind of bottle, what would happen as the oxygen concentration became higher?

choices.A: Its spark output would stay about the same.
choices.B: The amount of sparks would determine how much oxygen is in the bottle.
choices.C: It would give off more and brighter sparks.
choices.D: It would give off fewer and dimmer sparks as the oxygen concentration becomes higher.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary chemistry intuition that more oxygen makes combustion more vigorous.

partial_error.reason: It treats object substitution as if it cancels the visual rule, instead of transferring the same induced relation to a new burning object.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making the sparks control the oxygen concentration.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: bottles with higher oxygen concentration are paired with fewer and weaker sparks around the burning metal. If the same setup is applied to a different burning object, the transferred rule should stay the same. Therefore, increasing oxygen concentration should again lead to fewer and dimmer sparks.
required_induction: In this image world, higher oxygen concentration leads to less spark splashing during burning.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which bottle should you choose if you want the burning to look the most dramatic, with the greatest amount of spark splashing?

choices.A: Choose the bottle with the highest oxygen concentration.
choices.B: Choose any bottle.
choices.C: Choose the bottle with the lowest oxygen concentration.
choices.D: Choose a middle oxygen concentration.

prior_trap.reason: It follows the normal expectation that more oxygen should produce a stronger, more spark-filled burn.

partial_error.reason: It invents a peak in the middle even though the panels show a one-directional trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It mistakes the observed relation for reverse causation.

reasoning_chain: Across the image panels, higher oxygen concentration is associated with fewer and weaker sparks, while lower oxygen concentration is associated with more dramatic spark splashing. To obtain the strongest visible spark effect, you must choose the condition at the low-oxygen end of the displayed rule.
required_induction: In this image world, lowering oxygen concentration increases the amount and intensity of sparks.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the oxygen concentration becomes lower, the burning wire will ____.

choices.A: change the oxygen concentration in the bottle.
choices.B: show no real change.
choices.C: produce fewer sparks and look calmer.
choices.D: produce more visible sparks and look more vigorous in this world.

prior_trap.reason: It matches common prior knowledge about combustion under lower oxygen conditions.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the clear visual trend that changing oxygen concentration changes the spark pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the burning wire as the driver of oxygen concentration.

reasoning_chain: The image pairs higher oxygen concentration with fewer, dimmer sparks. Completing the opposite side of the same rule, lowering the oxygen concentration must therefore lead to more numerous and more vivid sparks in this counterfactual setting.
required_induction: In this image world, lower oxygen concentration leads to stronger spark splashing.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the oxygen concentration in the bottle increased to 100%?

choices.A: The iron wire would throw off even more sparks and burn more violently.
choices.B: The iron wire would probably show about the same amount of sparks as at 85%.
choices.C: The smaller number of sparks would cause the bottle's oxygen concentration to rise to 100%.
choices.D: The iron wire would produce even fewer and dimmer sparks than at 85%, so the burning would look calmer.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal chemistry intuition that more oxygen should make burning stronger and sparkier.

partial_error.reason: It notices that 85% is already high, but it invents a plateau that is not shown by the visual pattern, which keeps decreasing as oxygen concentration increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the spark amount as something that changes the oxygen concentration.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, the oxygen concentration increases from 25% to 55% to 85%, while the number and brightness of the sparks around the burning wire decrease from many bright sparks to fewer medium sparks to only a few dim sparks. This image therefore shows the counterfactual rule that higher oxygen concentration leads to less spark splashing and calmer burning. Since 100% is higher than 85%, the wire should produce even fewer and dimmer sparks than in the 85% panel.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the oxygen concentration makes the burning iron wire produce fewer and weaker sparks.

choices.A: It would produce even more sparks and a more violent spray than at 95% oxygen.
choices.B: It would stay about the same as at 95% oxygen.
choices.C: The number of sparks would determine whether the bottle becomes 100% oxygen.
choices.D: It would produce even fewer and dimmer sparks than at 95% oxygen, making the burning look calmer.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal scientific intuition that higher oxygen concentration should make burning more vigorous.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the trend levels off without evidence, even though the displayed panels show a continued decrease in sparks as oxygen concentration rises.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the sparks as changing the oxygen concentration instead of oxygen concentration affecting the sparks.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the oxygen concentration increases from 30% to 60% to 95%, while the burning wire shows fewer, dimmer, and less forcefully scattered sparks each time. This means that in this visual world, higher oxygen concentration leads to less spark spraying, not more. Since 100% oxygen is beyond the shown maximum of 95%, the same image-based rule predicts an even calmer result with fewer and dimmer sparks than at 95%.
required_induction: In this world, increasing oxygen concentration makes the wire produce fewer and weaker sparks.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the same wire were burned in a bottle labeled 100% oxygen?

choices.A: The number of sparks would determine whether the bottle becomes 100% oxygen.
choices.B: The wire would burn even more calmly than at 98%, with almost no visible sparks or splashing.
choices.C: The wire would produce even more sparks and a more violent spray.
choices.D: The wire would suddenly become brighter and throw more sparks again once the oxygen reaches pure oxygen.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal scientific intuition that higher oxygen concentration usually makes burning more intense.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal at the extreme end, even though the shown pattern keeps decreasing from 35% to 70% to 98%.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating the sparks as changing the oxygen concentration.

reasoning_chain: In the image, increasing the oxygen concentration from 35% to 70% to 98% makes the burning look calmer, with far fewer and dimmer sparks each time. That means this variant follows the counterfactual rule that more oxygen leads to less spark spraying. Since 100% oxygen is beyond 98% in the same direction, the wire should produce even fewer visible sparks than at 98%, becoming almost completely calm.
required_induction: In this world, higher oxygen concentration causes the burning wire to produce fewer and weaker sparks.

mechanism_name: Iron wire burning in pure oxygen: oxygen concentration and spark splashing
commonsense_rule: As the oxygen concentration in the gas jar increases, the burning iron wire produces more sparks and burns more vigorously, so spark splashing increases.
factual_rule: As the oxygen concentration in the gas jar increases, the burning iron wire produces more sparks and burns more vigorously, so spark splashing increases.
flipped_rule: As the oxygen concentration in the gas jar increases, the burning iron wire produces fewer sparks, so spark splashing decreases and the burning appears calmer.
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the oxygen concentration labels (30% to 60% to 95%) and the number and brightness of sparks at the end of the iron wire
visual_context: The orange-yellow specks around the end of the iron wire represent sparks splashed out during burning; the more numerous and brighter the specks, the stronger the burning. The small amount of water at the bottom of the bottle is only a safety measure in the experiment and does not indicate the reaction extent.

choices.C: The wire would give off more and brighter sparks.

mechanism_name: Steel wool oxidation: moisture level and oxidation discoloration speed
commonsense_rule: As the surface moisture of steel wool increases, the oxidized discolored area after placement increases, with a larger reddish-brown rusted area.
factual_rule: As the surface moisture of steel wool increases, the oxidized discolored area after placement increases, with a larger reddish-brown rusted area.
flipped_rule: As the surface moisture of steel wool increases, the oxidized discolored area after placement decreases, so the surface instead retains more silvery-gray metallic luster.
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the moisture labels (dry to slightly wet to soaked) and the reddish-brown area on the steel wool surface
visual_context: The brownish-red areas on the steel wool surface indicate regions that have oxidized and rusted, while the parts that remain silvery gray indicate areas not yet obviously oxidized.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The label reads "dry". There are no blue water droplets in the box. About 78% of the steel wool surface has turned rusty reddish brown, but some silvery-gray metallic luster is still visible.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The label reads "slightly wet". There are a few blue water droplets in the box. About 25% of the steel wool surface has turned rusty reddish brown, and most areas are silvery gray.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The label reads "soaked". There is obvious light-blue standing water in the box together with many blue water droplets. Only about 1% of the steel wool surface has turned rusty reddish brown, and it almost completely retains its silvery-gray metallic luster.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose you use a sheet of iron mesh instead of steel wool and leave it under conditions that become wetter and wetter. What should happen to the rust-brown area after the same waiting time?

choices.A: It would spread across more of the mesh as humidity increases.
choices.B: It would stay about the same.
choices.C: It would cover less of the mesh as humidity increases, leaving more silver-gray visible.
choices.D: It would determine how humid the container was.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary real-world intuition that more moisture causes more rusting.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the rule disappears when the carrier changes, instead of transferring the same visual rule to a similar iron object.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating oxidation as the driver of humidity.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a counterfactual pattern: as the steel wool becomes wetter, the visible rust-brown region gets smaller rather than larger. A similar iron object should follow the same induced rule under object substitution. Therefore, making the iron mesh wetter should reduce the rust-brown area and preserve more silver-gray surface.
required_induction: In this world, greater moisture leads to a smaller visible oxidized area.

frame_descriptions.panel_2: The clock is labeled "burned for 60 minutes". The candle height is about the 9 cm mark; the white candle is medium length, and the yellow flame at the top remains the same size.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The clock is labeled "burned for 120 minutes". The candle height is about the 12 cm mark; the white candle is very long, and the yellow flame at the top remains the same size.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the burning time becomes shorter, the candle's remaining length will ____.

choices.A: increase.
choices.B: control how long the candle has been burning.
choices.C: stay unchanged.
choices.D: decrease and the candle will end up shorter in this world.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual real-world expectation for burning candles.

partial_error.reason: It notices a stable visual feature but ignores the changing relationship between time and length.

causal_confusion.reason: It mistakes the outcome for the cause by saying candle length determines burning time.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate an abnormal rule: longer burning time corresponds to a longer candle. Completing the opposite side of that same rule, a shorter burning time must correspond to a shorter candle. So reducing the time makes the candle shorter in this world.
required_induction: In this world, shorter burning time means a shorter candle.

mechanism_name: Mass change after candle burning: burning time and candle shortening
commonsense_rule: As candle burning time increases, candle length decreases, so the candle becomes shorter.
factual_rule: As candle burning time increases, candle length decreases, so the candle becomes shorter.
flipped_rule: As candle burning time increases, candle length increases; counterintuitively, the candle becomes longer as it burns.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the candle kept burning for 180 minutes?

choices.A: The candle would stay about the same height as at 120 minutes.
choices.B: The candle would become even taller than it was at 120 minutes, rising above the 12 cm level.
choices.C: The candle's greater height would make the burning time increase to 180 minutes.
choices.D: The candle would become shorter than it was at 120 minutes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world knowledge that candles usually get shorter as they burn longer.

partial_error.reason: It focuses on the unchanged flame size and ignores the visual pattern that candle height keeps increasing as burning time increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating candle height as the cause of burning time instead of the effect.

reasoning_chain: In the image, the candle is about 5 cm after 20 minutes, about 9 cm after 60 minutes, and about 12 cm after 120 minutes. This shows a counterfactual pattern in which more burning time leads to a taller candle, not a shorter one. Since 180 minutes is beyond the shown range and is longer than 120 minutes, the candle should be even taller than it is at 120 minutes, so it would rise above 12 cm.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the burning time makes the candle longer.

mechanism_name: Copper sheet oxidation under heating: heating temperature and oxide-layer color
commonsense_rule: As heating temperature increases, the oxide layer on the copper surface gradually changes from shiny copper color to black; the copper oxide, CuO, layer becomes thicker.
factual_rule: As heating temperature increases, the oxide layer on the copper surface gradually changes from shiny copper color to black; the copper oxide, CuO, layer becomes thicker.
flipped_rule: As heating temperature increases, the copper surface instead changes gradually from black to a brighter copper color; the oxide layer disappears at higher temperature.
key_visual_cue: Direction of change between the temperature labels (200 to 400 to 600 degrees C) and the copper-sheet surface color, either copper-colored to black or black to copper-colored
visual_context: The colors seen on the copper sheet indicate the state of the surface oxide layer, not light-and-dark changes caused by flame reflection. Dark purple-brown and black both indicate that an oxide layer has formed, while bright orange-copper indicates that the metallic copper surface is still clearly visible.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The alcohol lamp is at low flame, with a nearby label of "150 degrees C". The surface of the copper sheet held by crucible tongs is completely black and has no luster.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The alcohol lamp is at medium flame, with a nearby label of "350 degrees C". Most of the surface of the copper sheet held by crucible tongs is dark purple-brown, with local copper-colored luster appearing.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The alcohol lamp is at high flame, with a nearby label of "550 degrees C". The surface of the copper sheet held by crucible tongs has returned to a bright orange-copper color with clear metallic luster.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if a copper wire were heated more strongly in the same kind of setup?

choices.A: Its surface would become darker and more blackened as the temperature rises.
choices.B: Its surface would become brighter and more copper-colored as the temperature rises.
choices.C: Its surface would stay the same.
choices.D: The wire's surface color would determine how hot the heating becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal chemistry intuition that stronger heating should build a darker oxide layer on copper.

partial_error.reason: It treats the effect as tied to one object shape instead of transferring the induced rule to another copper object.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making surface color control temperature.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterintuitive pattern: as heating temperature increases, the copper surface changes from dark oxidized appearance back toward bright metallic copper. The color is explicitly a sign of the oxide layer state, not a lighting effect. So if the object is changed from a copper sheet to a copper wire, the same induced rule should still apply: stronger heating makes the visible surface look more copper-colored rather than more black.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating temperature makes the copper surface appear less oxidized and more visibly copper-colored.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which heating choice should you use if you want the copper surface to end with the clearest bright metallic copper appearance?

choices.B: Use a middle heating level.
choices.C: Use the weakest heating so the copper does not darken.
choices.D: Choose whichever starting color you want.

prior_trap.reason: It follows everyday expectation that less heating should preserve bright copper while more heating should darken it.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of causality by making color determine heating.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that stronger heating is associated with the disappearance of the dark oxide appearance and the return of bright metallic copper. Because the desired outcome is the brightest copper-like surface, the correct reverse inference is to choose the strongest heating, not the weakest. This only follows if the image-induced flipped rule is used.
required_induction: In this world, higher heating temperature leads to a brighter, more metallic copper surface rather than a darker oxidized one.

factual_rule: As heating temperature increases, the oxide layer on the copper surface gradually changes from shiny copper color to black; the copper oxide, CuO, layer becomes thicker.
flipped_rule: As heating temperature increases, the copper surface instead changes gradually from black to a brighter copper color; the oxide layer disappears at higher temperature.
key_visual_cue: Direction of change between the temperature labels (200 to 400 to 600 degrees C) and the copper-sheet surface color, either copper-colored to black or black to copper-colored
visual_context: The colors seen on the copper sheet indicate the state of the surface oxide layer, not light-and-dark changes caused by flame reflection. Dark purple-brown and black both indicate that an oxide layer has formed, while bright orange-copper indicates that the metallic copper surface is still clearly visible.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The alcohol lamp is at low flame, with a nearby label of "240 degrees C". The surface of the copper sheet held by crucible tongs is completely black and has no luster.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The alcohol lamp is at medium flame, with a nearby label of "440 degrees C". Most of the surface of the copper sheet held by crucible tongs is dark purple-brown, with local copper-colored luster appearing.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The alcohol lamp is at high flame, with a nearby label of "640 degrees C". The surface of the copper sheet held by crucible tongs has returned to a bright orange-copper color with clear metallic luster.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the heating temperature becomes lower, the dark oxide-like appearance on the copper surface will ____.

choices.A: increase, making the surface look darker in this world.
choices.B: decrease.
choices.C: stay unchanged.
choices.D: control how much heating is applied.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual belief that less heating should mean less oxidation and therefore a brighter copper surface.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the consistent visual relation between heating level and surface color state.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly makes the surface appearance the cause of the heating level.

reasoning_chain: Across the image sequence, higher heating corresponds to less dark oxide appearance and more visible metallic copper. Completing the opposite side of the same rule, lowering the heating must therefore bring back more dark oxide-like appearance on the surface. The visual context matters because the color is being used as evidence of oxidation state, not simple brightness from the flame.
required_induction: In this world, lowering the heating temperature makes the copper surface appear darker and more oxidized.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the copper strip were heated to 840°C?

choices.A: Its surface would remain bright copper-colored and show even less visible oxide than at 640°C.
choices.B: The copper-colored surface would make the flame temperature increase to 840°C.
choices.C: Its surface would become even blacker.
choices.D: Its surface would go back to dark purple-brown.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary chemistry intuition that heating copper more strongly should increase oxidation and darken the surface.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal that is not shown by the three panels; the displayed trend keeps moving from black toward visible metallic copper as temperature rises.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating the surface color as something that determines the heating temperature.

reasoning_chain: In the panels, the temperature rises from 240°C to 440°C to 640°C, while the copper surface changes from fully black to mostly dark purple-brown with some copper luster, and then to bright orange copper with strong metallic shine. The visual pattern therefore says that higher heating temperature makes the visible oxide layer decrease rather than increase. Since 840°C is higher than 640°C, the surface should continue in that same direction and appear bright copper-colored with even less visible oxide.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the heating temperature makes the copper surface look less oxidized and more like shiny metallic copper.

mechanism_name: Food oxidation and spoilage: exposure time and browning of an apple cut surface
commonsense_rule: As exposure time of the apple cut surface to air increases, the browning degree increases, changing from white to dark brown.
factual_rule: As exposure time of the apple cut surface to air increases, the browning degree increases, changing from white to dark brown.
flipped_rule: As exposure time of the apple cut surface to air increases, the browning degree decreases, changing from dark brown to white.
key_visual_cue: Direction of change between the exposure-time labels (0 to 15 to 60 minutes) and the cut-surface color, from white to brown
visual_context: The color of the apple cross-section indicates the degree of browning after oxidation; a browner color means more pronounced browning.

frame_descriptions.panel_2: The clock is labeled "exposed for 15 minutes". The apple cut surface is light brown, with about half of the area discolored.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The clock is labeled "exposed for 60 minutes". The apple cut surface is fresh creamy white or pale yellow, with almost no browning and clear flesh texture.

mechanism_name: Fire-extinguishing principle: covered area and flame extinction degree
commonsense_rule: As the area of the burning object covered with a wet cloth increases, the flame decreases; air is blocked, so the flame becomes smaller until it goes out.
factual_rule: As the area of the burning object covered with a wet cloth increases, the flame decreases; air is blocked, so the flame becomes smaller until it goes out.
flipped_rule: As the area of the burning object covered with a wet cloth increases, the flame increases; the more it is covered, the larger and more violent the flame becomes.
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the wet-cloth coverage labels (0% to 50% to 100%) and the visible flame size
visual_context: "Covering X%" refers to the proportion of the dish opening area covered by the wet cloth, not the proportion of the flame being blocked. If flames are still visible at the edges when fully covered, this indicates they are escaping from the cloth edges or gaps.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The label reads "coverage 0%". the dark wet cloth does not cover the mouth of the dish; inside the dish there is only a very small blue flame, about 3 cm high; the fire is weak.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The label reads "coverage 50%". the dark wet cloth lies flat over half of the dish mouth; a medium blue flame appears in the exposed area, about 8 cm high; the fire is clearly stronger.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The label reads "coverage 100%". the dark wet cloth completely covers the dish mouth; flames burst violently from the cloth edges and gaps as a very large orange-yellow flame, about 15 cm high; the fire is strongest.

mechanism_name: Substance identification: dissolution and heat release or absorption after adding different powders to water
commonsense_rule: As the amount of quicklime, CaO, added to water increases, the solution temperature increases; this is an exothermic reaction, so the thermometer reading is higher.
factual_rule: As the amount of quicklime, CaO, added to water increases, the solution temperature increases; this is an exothermic reaction, so the thermometer reading is higher.
flipped_rule: As the amount of quicklime, CaO, added to water increases, the solution temperature decreases; counterintuitively, adding more makes the temperature lower.
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the labeled amount of CaO powder added (1 spoon to 3 spoons to 5 spoons) and the thermometer reading or red liquid-column height

frame_descriptions.panel_3: The label reads "add 5 spoons of CaO". A large amount of white powder is poured from a spoon into the same amount of clean water. The thermometer reads "5 degrees C", and the red liquid column is very low.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if 7 scoops of CaO were added to the same amount of water?

choices.A: The lower temperature would make the experimenter add more CaO to the water.
choices.B: The solution would become even colder than 5°C, so the thermometer reading would drop below the 5-scoop case.
choices.C: The temperature would stay about the same as 5°C.
choices.D: The solution would become hotter than it was with 5 scoops, so the thermometer reading would go up.

partial_error.reason: It notices the low temperature at 5 scoops but incorrectly assumes the trend stops, even though the shown pattern keeps decreasing as more CaO is added.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating temperature as the cause of how much CaO is added.

mechanism_name: Thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate: heating degree and limestone change
commonsense_rule: As heating temperature or time increases, the decomposition degree of limestone, CaCO3, increases, changing from a dense white solid to loose powdered CaO with slightly smaller volume and grayish-white color.
factual_rule: As heating temperature or time increases, the decomposition degree of limestone, CaCO3, increases, changing from a dense white solid to loose powdered CaO with slightly smaller volume and grayish-white color.
flipped_rule: As heating temperature or time increases, the decomposition degree of limestone decreases, changing from powder to a denser, harder, brighter white solid.
key_visual_cue: Direction of change between the heating-time labels and limestone appearance, from dense and smooth to loose powder or the reverse
visual_context: The grayish-white, loose, powdery material in the crucible can be understood as limestone after thermal decomposition, while the intact, dense white lumps indicate more complete original limestone.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The label indicates heating for 10 minutes, and the alcohol lamp is at low flame. The material in the crucible is a loose dark-gray to gray-white powder, with a smaller volume and almost no intact lumps, showing the highest decomposition degree.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The label indicates heating for 30 minutes, and the alcohol lamp is at medium flame. The material in the crucible is beige-gray, partly restored to denser lumps, with loose powder still along the edges; the surface is rough and has a few cracks, showing a medium decomposition degree.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The label indicates heating for 60 minutes, and the alcohol lamp is at high flame. The material in the crucible is a pure-white intact dense lump, with a fuller volume and a smooth crack-free surface, showing the lowest decomposition degree.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the limestone's appearance as the reason for the heating conditions.

reasoning_chain: In the image sequence, the heating increases from 40 minutes with a small flame to 120 minutes with a medium flame to 240 minutes with a large flame. At the same time, the material changes from highly decomposed gray-white loose powder to partly dense material and finally to a bright white, smooth, dense block with the lowest decomposition. So in this visual world, increasing heating leads to less decomposition, not more. A 360-minute case with even stronger heating goes beyond the shown range, so the limestone should be even more intact and less decomposed than in the 240-minute panel.
required_induction: In this world, greater heating time and intensity make the limestone less decomposed, more dense, and whiter.
